# Transcrição: 02-Where Humor Hides

- **Fonte:** 02-Where Humor Hides
- **Duração:** 07:43
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

All right, I'm about to teach you five strategies for being funny. But I want you to know sort of where to find the places to apply those five strategies. Essentially, where does humor hide in language? There are some places where humor is really easy to find and other places where it's impossible to find. So for example, the best place to be funny in any story or talk or speech or giving is when there's the greatest level of optionality or choice. In other words, when you have a choice over what to say, your chances of being funny increase exponentially. When you are trapped with facts that you cannot manipulate, the ability to be funny is much harder. So for example, when it comes time to describe something, if I need to describe a cheeseburger to you, I have enormous optionality. I can choose any aspect of the cheeseburger to describe. I can choose its temperature, its size, its components. I can choose the taste of the cheeseburger, the physical appearance of the cheeseburger. When we describe things, we can choose any of the aspects of those things and therefore we have enormous optionality. It allows us to be funny. When I'm stuck with facts, I can't do the same. When we engage in internal dialogue in a story, when we're reporting on what we're thinking, we have enormous optionality. We're not actually reporting on dialogue, which we can't control. What someone says is what someone says. Internal dialogue, what were we thinking? We have enormous optionality. We can say almost anything. We can make things up that we were thinking. We can say whatever we want in internal dialogue in any way that we want to produce a laugh. So internal dialogue provides lots of choices where actual dialogue provides no choices. We're trapped with the words that people said. We can report them and maybe we can say them in a funnier way or maybe we can chop them off a little bit, cut the speaker off to make it funny, but we're really stuck in most cases. So again, internal dialogue, lots of optionality, actual dialogue, not a lot. Backstory is a great place to find humor. If I'm telling a story and I need to teach you that when I was a kid, I was irresponsible, you can imagine I have a wealth of choices at my fingertips. I can choose basically anything from my childhood that demonstrates that I'm an irresponsible kid. I'll just choose the ones that are funny. I'll just choose those moments from my past that are most humorous and also serve the

## [03:03 - 06:07]

story I'm trying to tell. Whenever we're engaged in backstory, which is teaching an audience about who we once were, whether the backstory takes place a day ago, a month ago, or a year ago, it increases our optionality. It increases the choices we have. So whenever we're speaking about the past and pulling in the past to bring the story that we're telling to the greatest clarity possible, bring the funny things in. Bring in those moments from your past where you were stupid or ridiculous. Bring in the embarrassing moments, the shameful moments. Bring in the dialogue that you spoke that you couldn't believe you spoke, that ridiculous thing you said that still makes people laugh today. Backstory has enormous optionality and allows you to be funny. Any time in a story or a speech when you have to provide a list, that's also an excellent opportunity to be funny. For example, if I had to give you five ways that my son wastes time, how he makes me crazy about how he spends his time and how slowly he moves through the world, I'll choose some that are really meaningful, some that actually have an emotional impact on me. Some of those frustrating ways that he moves through this world that make me crazy, but I'm definitely going to include at least one that's funny. I'm going to pick at least one of the ways that he wastes time that will make you laugh. I'll probably put it last because the funny thing should probably come last most of the time depending on what's following the list. Whenever you have to provide a list, a list of attributes, a list of qualities, a list of ingredients, all of these things, it's so easy to throw in a funny one. It's so easy to make people laugh in the midst of that list because again, you have so many choices. You can add almost anything to that list so you choose the funny thing. Put it in the list, put it in the right place, it'll produce enormous laughs. The last place that's easy to be funny is whenever you're having sort of an intense emotional response and a talk. Essentially it's called an act out in comedy, which is to say, if you're upset in a story, act upset in the story and that will make people laugh. If you are having a really unfortunate reaction to something, when you're really angry at people, when you're really upset with the world, if you can produce that response while telling your story, if you can recreate that response, that will often produce a laugh. If you're really angry at someone in line because they're taking too long, rather than saying I'm really angry at the person in line because they're taking too long, produce the response instead. I can't believe how long this person is taking. She's opened up her wallet and it's like there's rabbits inside. She's like shoving the rabbits aside to try to find the money that's buried at the bottom of the wallet. How hard can it be to find your checkbook and why are you using a checkbook because it's

## [06:07 - 07:43]

2023 rather than just saying it, act it out and it becomes funny. Moments of intense emotional response, moments where you're frustrated or angry, even moments when you're really sad, when you're losing your mind, moments of confusion, all of these can be really funny if you choose to act them out, reenact the experience for the benefit of the audience. These are all places where humor hides in stories and in speeches and in talks of all kinds, but essentially it's when you have maximum choice. When you don't have to get trapped in plot or dialogue, when you don't have to report on numbers and scientific data, when you really have choices, what I want to pull in, what I want to leave out, those are the moments when you get to be funny. As you start to think about the strategies that I'm going to offer you today, think about where they will be hiding in the stories you tell and the speeches you deliver. You're often going to be found in the moments when you have maximum optionality, when you have a choice over how to describe or elaborate or list. Whenever you have a chance to bring in backstory, all of those places are going to be the spots where you can land those strategies most effectively. It's going to be sort of the glaring, easy to find. This is where I can be funny spots in a story. That is where humor hides. Humor hides when you have the most choices at hand.
